speaker: interviewee
text: I can-- we-- I can talk?

speaker: interviewer
text: One second.

speaker: interviewer
text: One minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four-- survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation with survivor, David Klebanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more about Dr. [NON-ENGLISH]-- Dr.--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his real name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, his name was Krepsbach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Krepsbach.

speaker: interviewee
text: Krepsbach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave him that nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nickname when he was in a camp-- not a Jewish camp. He was camp-- in a camp Mauthausen, where Poles, Czechs, Russians were there, even the Italians, and sometimes few French people were in these camps. And that was, you know, with-- with-- in Mauthausen. And he was a doctor there. And he became famous among the-- the-- he came already with the reputation with the SS people. He came, and the SS people, oh, Krepsbach [NON-ENGLISH]. We called him [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How old a man was he at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- Krepsbach were around 45, 48.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to work with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he would come. He came, basically, to inspect me twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask now-- what happened with the woman with the infection--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the woman who was in the hospital? You didn't finish telling me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next day-- yes, this is next day. I was informed Wiesner is her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wiesner is the right hand of this Dr. Krepsbach. He is not a doctor. But he's-- in administrative capacity-- came. I was-- went out, and I see he going to the second floor-- I after him. And he knew exactly where she's located. And he-- I am behind him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he know?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spies-- informers were plenty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe Jews. I don't know. I suspected it must be one Jew could say to-- from the-- from the-- from the guard-- guard, oh, this woman is now on the second floor. And guards were-- were in there. Oh, it is bed-- bed on the left side.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rooms were there, by the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: One in this revere. In my revere there was only one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he comes. And now this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Get up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Told the woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Get up. And she understood very well. She got up. She has a small bundle of things. She took the bundles with her. And he took her on the truck and brought to Kaiserwald. I have my informers what is going on in Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: And next day she was taken to a place outside of the-- of the camp, and didn't return-- means they killed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old a woman was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: 28, 29, pretty nice-- a working woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever abort-- perform abortions on teenage girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, with where-- with whether I performed--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Spilve, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Spilve, teenage-- teenage-- fourteen-- and the teacher, they were killed. You didn't have of this age.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were the women then in your--

speaker: interviewee
text: This one-- 28, 29. The women were from 18 up to 40.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, shortly, that is an episode to mention. And the Russians are coming closer. And we are very tense what will be.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: That is approximately August '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you first-- were you with your wife? Your first wife was still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes I was with my wife-- yes-- my wife, obviously. I brought--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I brought her to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, was she your assistant in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was all the time helping me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Oh, she was a doctor, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Too, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I remember now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened there in 1944, August?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started-- we don't know. Will they liquidate us now or they will take us? The 2,000 Hungarian women, they brought-- they took them one day out, and we didn't hear about them missing or they were liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now will they us liquidate, or will they transport us to Germany? We didn't know. We were very tense. We don't know any-- have any information.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were few children in the age 10, 12 in our camp-- still survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they let-- what were those children doing in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know-- nothing. But the children ordered by the commandant of the camp to come together. Some mothers said we-- we stay with the children. And it's all right. Go with the children. They were taken, and they were killed somewhere-- disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not in-- not on-- not in Spilve?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Spilve. In Spilve-- this is in Kaiserwald. They have the place in Spilve where the places there where they were killing the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people killed in Spilve?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Spilve, no. No. We don't have any-- any cases of human killing in Spilve-- oh, one case.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you want to-- [LAUGHS] --want to know, yes. One case, it was a Czech-- intelligent guy. I don't recall what he was-- electrician-- but an intelligent guy. And now it is already August-- August, '44. See, he had somewhere contracts-- see, I don't know what. And he-- on one day he disappeared. He ran away. But he was caught. He was brought to our camp. And in our camp, he was hanged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was standing by. I had to stand by. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] The commandant, at that time, was a name, [NON-ENGLISH]-- terrible name from Dachau. He was pretty good to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he good to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: One different-- one-- one-- in all-- I had to come to him and ask him, you know, I need medication. And he would keep me. Then we make conversations. And here the door-- the door opens-- not the door-- the gate opens. And the 2,000 Hungarian women came in. And they looks and looks. And I say-- look too, you know. And he says, when I was in Dachau, I was strict. I considered all the people, they're enemies of the Dutch side. There was no Jews in Dachau, basically, at the very end.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what can I say about these women? I say, he-- he pushed my leg. That my-- my idea. He pushed my leg. He wants to say, you know, that I am-- I am-- I am different. But I am playing with him, you know. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He left. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He came to me somewhere in end of September, mid of September, or the beginning of September. Klebanow, I'm going with 400 women away. I need a doctor. Would you like to go with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, at that time, I had my wife. I had my sister-in-law from Vienna. I had my cousin's daughter. I had some family. I say, yes. And I will take all your family. He knew it. I say, I am afraid-- turned around and left.

speaker: interviewer
text: After liberation, I am meeting-- I went to a Dr-- Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], who was also in concentration camp-- in main camp, Kaiserwald. There I meet another widow of a doctor. And when she heard the word, Klebanow-- [NON-ENGLISH]. She said who said always my friend Klebanow-- Dr. Klebanow? I say, if somebody, it must be Blatterspiel.

speaker: interviewee
text: She say, yes, Blatterspiel very frequent to [NON-ENGLISH]. She were-- went with these 400 woman. He will always repeat, my friend, Dr. Klebanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to that group of women?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- two days before the Russians, he send away all the guards. One day before the Russians, he told-- gathered them-- first-- the 400 people-- please, don't show up in the windows. Nobody has to see that-- that you are here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of place were they in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a camp, camp with-- with barracks, and et cetera. Don't show up. Tomorrow are the Russians here-- turned around, and left. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He saved their lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, he saved their lives, yes. Or in other places, other places there are-- it was the most brutal cases was a place [NON-ENGLISH], where my friend Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] perished, where the SS put the-- commanded them to put the wood, and to lay down, and then to kill them. And so, what-- whoever was in-- in the-- in the camp, you know, they killed on the-- on the wood. They burned them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back to-- to your camp. I want to ask you just one question, though. Was there anything-- did you provide for the people in your camp in-- that they were men and women-- any kind of contraceptive devices?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no contraceptive devices. The women basically lived in the-- in women compound and the men in men compound.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how-- did you and your wife live together in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in the-- I have a small revere. It means a small hospital. And the-- she-- my wife was with the dentist of this-- we-- I had a dentist.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were able to live together in the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have any sex.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Why not? No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She worked--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your-- you choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: She worked with me. What?

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me what happened after August '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is-- we are in September. I-- he left-- Blatterspiel-- with the 400 women. We left. The 2,000 Hungarian women were taken away-- disappeared. And we don't know whether we will-- what will be with us. And on one day, we're ordered to go to the main camp, Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- by trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there now in your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: You must-- it was around, I will say probably around 4,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everyone was taken from the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the--

speaker: interviewer
text: From Spilve?

speaker: interviewee
text: --from Spilve, everything-- we was taken there. It is already-- it was fluctuating. It was a-- a big group of us sent to another camp-- another camp to-- to work from this over there. At the end, we had in Spilve around 400 or 500 people. And with this, we came to the main camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Main camp-- that was already typical concentration camp with gas camera-- gas--

speaker: interviewer
text: Gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: --gas chamber. With all this, you know, we got, you know, new numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your number-- where did you get a number first?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that? Where did you get that number?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? In Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have that on your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had it on my-- on my-- on my this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Chest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Spilve-- only who were in Auschwitz, where-- where they had on the arms-- only Spilve-- only Auschwitz. All other camps didn't have any-- any of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me again, slowly, the number that you got in--

speaker: interviewee
text: 96043. That is 96,043. That was my now number. From this 96,000, only maybe 10,000 were in the camp. The 83, they were not liberated-- maybe few. Single persons were liberated. They perished in this camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. The name of the camp, again, was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stutthof, OK. All right. When did you get to Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stutthof, we came beginning of-- of October.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Kaiserwald only, you know, the-- from there I took-- took us on a-- on a boat. And they brought us to Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your wife? Where was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also-- no. She went to Stutthof, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know you were a doctor at that-- in Kaiserwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in my camp-- career as a doctor ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here I am a simple, simple guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simple. Next, terrible conditions in the camp-- appells-- they-- appells, you know-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: I know-- counting.

speaker: interviewee
text: --yes. And that is hours and hours in the morning, hours and hours in the evening, rain or shine-- rain or whatever you want-- you know, everything. It was terrible. We were in the barracks. You know, it was three-- three tiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do in Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stutthof, when we came into Stutthof, basically, we didn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there food?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Food-- was there any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food-- got that-- means a piece of bread and in the evening, a water and soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Stutthof in-- I was remained for approximately four weeks

speaker: interviewer
text: Then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: A commando was-- was formed to go to work in the Port of Danzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you go-- were you in Danzig for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are in Danzig around 10 miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Danzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this camp, till the-- exactly 8th of February, '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the Russians came closer, and we were brought-- we went on a march. And finally, we landed in a camp called [NON-ENGLISH]. It is near a city in-- that is Pommern. That is a part of Germany. It is a state of Germany, Pommern. And we are brought near the city, Laumberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there for-- when we finally came-- few days-- not few days-- around two weeks. People from other camps joined us. And from other camps they brought typhus, paratyphus, paratyphus, typhoid, paratyphoid and typhus. Typhus was the most dangerous thing. And I with my friend, Kronsoln-- there were other doctors-- they are very afraid. We went to take care of these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you do that? Why weren't you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dear, I, all the time, was working with the people. It is not a doctor. I was-- I was a doctor, but I was-- therefore, you know, I-- I-- I am alive. Therefore, nobody, no one would said they've been warned about me. It was-- I was just one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? What-- what was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: If I didn't do it-- to be able to survive. I can-- so therefore, you know, whatever I can help them, I will help them. That was my attitude-- truly, 100%. And if you take in all the book, I give the book to [NON-ENGLISH], in a book after the war describing Riga, the [NON-ENGLISH] from Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the [NON-ENGLISH] means the destruction of-- of Riga-- the-- of Riga-- of Riga Jewry-- Jewry. In Germany, there is a big article about Spilve. And he, the man who wrote it, he didn't know me-- wrote, you know, on two pages about me, my behavior-- that I knew the mentality of the-- of the Germans, and even SS. If I have to talk to them, I have talked to militarily. I answered militarily. I had military-- military education. And they liked it. And-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --in this way, I could achieve much, much more. And so, that is the last of everything so long I was in Riga. Riga, 30th of September, that is finished. I-- I'm not a doctor anymore. I'm one of them. I went, you know, to work to-- to Port of Danzig, where we order to build a railroad in the Port of Danzig. It is a-- a branch of their railroad-- under supervision-- supervision. We had to bring, you know, only the heavy, heavy-- what goes under the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, tight. Here it is a metallic, trying to see your-- they use wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] And soaked in water, you know, how to say. It's so thick, you know, under this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very heavy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very heavy. We too-- we two had to bring it. And when I recall, I had right away pains here on my shoulder. And my backaches now is much more worse-- worse than my other problems, the-- the things the backaches from-- from the time when I was carrying these things. I still didn't believe that I will survive. I got-- I was attending these-- with people with-- coming, passing by, attending-- I took a whole school-- school for these people, you know, at least to bring them into warm conditions, to give them something resembled tea, water--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this still--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because they were dehydrated.

speaker: interviewer
text: --during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is already at the end. That is already-- already we are in the-- in the-- in the end of February, beginning of-- of-- of March-- and the beginning of March. That how we-- that was-- and I got--

speaker: interviewer
text: You got sick, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my-- my typhus. And I was-- when-- or an the order was to move further-- we were around 10, 12 people with typhus in a small barrack. And there were maybe 20-- small barrack. And we said, we cannot. We have no-- we were sure that they-- they will come and they will kill us. But it didn't happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Happens that just-- it came the 10th or 11th day of my sickness, the temperature comes rapidly down. And you feel suddenly much better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have medication for yourself at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No medication. I had only-- I only, you know, I was getting hot water and potatoes cooked with-- with the-- with the European-- European potatoes, you have to remove the skin. Because it contains-- it contains a gift called ptomaine. So we-- they were cooked with the skin. We-- all right, we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ptomine is a poisoning, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: --ptomine is a poisoning. So we just now remove-- remove the skin, and we ate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- What did it taste like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --it is-- my dear, the-- the-- here, during this night, you know, a Polish-- Polish prisoner were-- he was a-- comes running and throws us a piece of-- of horse's meat. Now, horse has been for us a delicacy. Because in this was the only-- only protein what we could get. It has a little taste. We know, because it could have so-called hippuric acid, it just-- acidity of this hippuric acid. But otherwise, it is-- it was perfect food what our body needed. And I recall that I took the pieces. And in the fire, you know, fire for me and for my friend, Dr. Kronsohn, we both have typhus. He has complication with the heart. I start to feel pains in my legs. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Next day, we-- somebody gets out and say there is a Russian officer outside-- a Russian soldier-- true-- a Russian soldier. And I hear tremendous amount of [NON-ENGLISH] Russian soldiers-- Russian army passing by, around 300 yards from us. You see a road. They paved the road. And they are passing, you know, one division-- a tremendous amount. I was-- was thinking for that-- oh, they are coming to liberate. No. That was not the easier intentions even at interested in us. We were near-- they were going towards Laumberg. Two, three miles from Laumberg was a-- a island, Peenemunde, where all the missiles there, German missiles, V1 and V2. You know, at the-- end of the war, they had missiles shooting into-- into London-- were-- were built. And there was a-- a race between the Russians and the English, English gun coming from-- from the north, Russians coming from the-- from the east. Everybody wanted, you know, to grab this installations-- installations. The Russians grabbed it. But the scientists were already in the hands of the Americans. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened to them. Where did you end up? Did you stay in Laumberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: --yes. With another friend, with my friend we moved, you know, we went to-- we went to Laumberg. We found an apartment. The Germans fled-- fled-- but we found their bread or something else to eat. And that was-- was, for us, the main problem-- food, food, food. We had enough food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here, you know, three Russians-- officers are coming-- Russians are coming in and started to talk like this. Do you have food? No, we don't have any food-- the mentality over here from concentration camp. We don't have any food. And they left and came a soldier with the whole sack, with all possible foods. Then he disappears and brings us different clothing. And after this comes one of the officers. Did you receive the food? Yes. Did you receive the clothing? Yes. And we start to thank you-- him. He says, why are you thanking us? We are Jews too. That was-- and here, you know, ends my time with concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to Laumberg. I spend a few days there with another friend, Paul [NON-ENGLISH]. He was veterinarian doctor. And we headed-- with-- went to a foot. He was much in a better condition than I am. He's a veterinarian. He went, you know, to-- to the-- he supplied right away with meat-- meat. But then Russians send epidemiological hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A hospital, a administration-- doctors-- doctors and administration to treat people with infectious conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Jews that they were treating?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, no. We're among-- in this-- in this the doctor of the-- this, the doctor, the chief doctor of this unit was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. The men who supplied us with all the food was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was also a Jew. But the--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Russian Jews. But there are other-- other doctors, not many-- four or five in this whole hospital. They were all Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to look for your wife at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I decided-- I-- they-- they brought me without my wife. My wife left-- left in-- in-- in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how do you-- what happened to your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now my-- my-- I got-- I was decided I stay here until I have information about my family. I cannot go to Bialystok, because the Poles take out Jews out of the-- of the trains, and they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you felt you were safer where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Safer here. I'm-- have Russians here. Poles came, but the Russians-- I'm with-- with the Russians. [NON-ENGLISH], you know, [NON-ENGLISH], I have friends-- friends are Russians officers. I treated them for gonorrhea. I got sulfa drugs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the drugs from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, from the-- from the hospital-- from the hospital and from the-- from the doctor's office-- office.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ending?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're almost finished. We have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ending. All right. That is, how to say, the story-- not the final story. It is, you know, right, you know. I-- I-- I found my wife dead in Eckernforde. It is a city, a port, in Western Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to finish now tape four. We'll continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to continue.
